annotations.label: Periodic function (informal)

anchor.snippet: A periodic function is a function that repeats its values at regular intervals.

annotations.note: `Function.Periodic f c := ∀ x, f (x + c) = f x` directly captures the informal notion of a function repeating its values at regular intervals.

annotations.label: Period

anchor.snippet: The length of the interval over which a periodic function repeats is called its period

annotations.note: The constant `c` in `Function.Periodic f c` is exactly the period.

annotations.label: Aperiodic function

anchor.snippet: Any function that is not periodic is called aperiodic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named `Aperiodic` predicate; one would simply write `¬ ∃ c ≠ 0, Function.Periodic f c`.

annotations.label: Periodic function (formal)

anchor.snippet: More formally, a function

annotations.note: The Mathlib definition `Function.Periodic f c := ∀ x, f (x + c) = f x` matches the formal Wikipedia definition.

annotations.label: Period of a function

anchor.snippet: A nonzero constant

annotations.note: The parameter `c` of `Function.Periodic f c` is the period; nonzeroness is supplied at usage sites rather than baked into the definition.

annotations.label: Integer multiples of a period are periods

anchor.snippet: any integer multiple

annotations.note: `Function.Periodic.int_mul` (and `Periodic.zsmul`, `Periodic.nsmul`) prove that integer multiples of a period are themselves periods.

annotations.label: Fundamental period

anchor.snippet: If there is a least positive period, it is called the fundamental period

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Function.minimalPeriod` for periodic points of iterated maps, but no `Function.Periodic`-style notion of least positive real period for general periodic functions.

annotations.label: Translational symmetry of graph

anchor.snippet: Geometrically, a periodic function's graph exhibits translational symmetry

annotations.note: The equation `f (x + c) = f x` in `Function.Periodic` is precisely the algebraic statement of translational symmetry of the graph by `c`.

annotations.label: Sawtooth wave (fractional part)

anchor.snippet: An example is the function

annotations.note: `Int.fract_periodic` proves `Function.Periodic Int.fract 1`, exactly the sawtooth fractional-part example.

annotations.label: Sine and cosine periodicity

anchor.snippet: The sine function and cosine function are periodic with a fundamental period of

annotations.note: `sin_periodic` and `cos_periodic` prove `Function.Periodic sin (2 * π)` and `Function.Periodic cos (2 * π)`; minimality of this period is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Dirichlet function

anchor.snippet: The Dirichlet function , for example, is periodic, with any nonzero rational number serving as a period.

annotations.note: A grep for `Dirichlet`/`Periodic` finds no Mathlib formalization of the indicator-of-rationals Dirichlet function or its periodicity.

annotations.label: Complex exponential periodicity

anchor.snippet: The complex exponential function is a periodic function with a purely imaginary period

annotations.note: `Complex.exp_periodic` states `Function.Periodic exp (2 * π * I)`, exactly the purely-imaginary period claim.

annotations.label: Elliptic (double-periodic) functions

anchor.snippet: A function on the complex plane can have two distinct, incommensurate periods without being a constant function.

annotations.note: The Weierstrass `℘` function is constructed in Mathlib for a `PeriodPair`, with `PeriodPair.weierstrassP_add_coe` establishing its double periodicity; no general `DoublyPeriodic` predicate is defined.

annotations.label: Repeated values under period

anchor.snippet: Periodic functions can take on values many times.

annotations.note: `Function.Periodic.not_injective` shows that a nonzero-period periodic function is not injective, i.e. takes values more than once.

annotations.label: Integral over one period invariant

anchor.snippet: its definite integral over any interval of length

annotations.note: `Function.Periodic.intervalIntegral_add_eq` proves that the integral over any interval of length one period is the same.

annotations.label: Period under horizontal scaling

anchor.snippet: is a non-zero real number such that

annotations.note: `Function.Periodic.const_mul` and `Function.Periodic.mul_const` (in `Mathlib.Algebra.Field.Periodic`) give the scaled period for `x ↦ f (a*x)` over a division semiring.

annotations.label: Combinations of periodic functions are periodic

anchor.snippet: Any function that is a combination of periodic functions with the same period is also periodic

annotations.note: Closure under addition/multiplication/division/scalar-multiplication with the same period is provided by `Periodic.add`, `Periodic.mul`, `Periodic.div`, `Periodic.smul`, etc.

annotations.label: Antiperiodic function

anchor.snippet: One subset of periodic functions is that of antiperiodic functions

annotations.note: `Function.Antiperiodic f c := ∀ x, f (x + c) = -f x` matches the Wikipedia definition exactly.

annotations.label: Antiperiodic implies periodic

anchor.snippet: the converse is not necessarily true

annotations.note: `Function.Antiperiodic.periodic` shows any `c`-antiperiodic function is `2 • c`-periodic.

annotations.label: Bloch-periodic function

anchor.snippet: Functions of this form are sometimes called Bloch-periodic in this context.

annotations.note: A grep for `Bloch` in `Mathlib/` returns no matches; no `Bloch`/quasi-periodic predicate is defined.

annotations.label: Quotient space domain for periodic functions

anchor.snippet: each element in

annotations.note: `Function.Periodic.lift` lifts a `c`-periodic function to a function on the quotient `α ⧸ AddSubgroup.zmultiples c`, exactly the quotient-space-domain construction.

annotations.label: Calculating period from frequency ratios

anchor.snippet: To find the period, T, first find the least common denominator of all the elements in the set.

annotations.note: Mathlib provides closure properties such as `Periodic.add_period` but no LCM/LCD-based algorithm for computing a common period from a set of frequency ratios.

annotations.label: Period of Western major scale

anchor.snippet: For set representing all notes of Western major scale

annotations.note: Mathlib has no music-theoretic formalization of the Western major scale or its period.

annotations.label: Period of major triad

anchor.snippet: For set representing all notes of a major triad

annotations.note: Mathlib has no music-theoretic formalization of major triads or their period.

annotations.label: Period of minor triad

anchor.snippet: For set representing all notes of a minor triad

annotations.note: Mathlib has no music-theoretic formalization of minor triads or their period.
